# Maps in R {#sec-mapsScripps}

![Tidyverse](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/795c039ba2520455d833b4034befc8cf360a70ba/558a5/diagrams/data-science-explore.png){#fig-TidyverseFramework}

## Install Packages

Packages are collections of functions that accomplish specialized tasks. To make a map today, we'll need to install three packages that are not part of base R.  

- `dplyr` - data manipulation 
- [`sf`]((https://r-spatial.github.io/sf/articles/sf1.html)) - spatial data manipulation
- [`leaflet`](https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/) - interactive, mobile-friendly maps 

Packages need to be installed locally the first time they are used.

```{r}
#| label: install packages
#| message: false
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| warnings: false

install.packages('dplyr')
install.packages('sf')
install.packages('leaflet')
```


Next, we load the packages to make sure they are available within the R environment.

```{r}
#| label: load  packages
#| warning: false
#| echo: true

library(dplyr)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)
```

## Acquiring Data

We will import a curated version of the [CalEnviroscreen4.0](https://oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen/report/calenviroscreen-40) data.

Coding Vocabulary
 
- `st_read()` reads geospatial data files.
- `st_transform()` alters coordinate reference system.
-  `<-` operator to assign to a variable
- `|>` pipe operator
- `crs` - coordinate reference system, where 4326 is [WGS84](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Geodetic_System)

```{r}
#| label: California Enviroscreen 4.0 SoCal dataset
#| warning: false
#| echo: true

#web url
URL.path <- 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/RadicalResearchLLC/EDVcourse/main/CalEJ4/CalEJ.geoJSON'

#read data and assign it 
SoCalEJ <- st_read(URL.path) |> 
  st_transform(crs = 4326) #change crs 

```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Pro tip: Always look at the data after import. Importing data is fraught with peril.
:::

```{r}
#| label: check header
#| warning: false
#| echo: true

head(SoCalEJ)

```


## Visualize the Data 

### Choose the visualization type and function

We'll use`leaflet()` for visualization for geospatial data today, and add at least one `add` function to display spatial information.

Vocabulary  

-   `leaflet()` - create a leaflet map  
-   `addTiles()` - add map tiles  
-   `addMarkers()` - add point markers  
-   `addPolygons()` - add filled polygons  
-   `addLegends()` - add a map legend   

### Write the code for a *basic* geospatial visualization

#### Example 1 - Basic Tile Map

@fig-mapBasic shows the standard leaflet map with a minimal reproducible example.

The code is simply two lines with two actionable functions.

```{r}
#| label: fig-mapBasic
#| fig-cap: A very basic Leaflet map
#| echo: true

leaflet() |> #call a leaflet map
  addTiles() #with default map tiles

```

#### Example 2. Add Environmental information

-   Make a leaflet map with the SoCalEJ dataset. It should look like @fig-CalEJ.

```{r}
#| label: fig-CalEJ
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map with Calenviroscreen 4.0
#| echo: true

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ)  #NEW

```

### Improve the Visualization

In the `addPolygons()` function, there are many `parameters` that can be modified.

-   `color`
-   `weight`
-   `opacity`
-   `fillColor`
-   `fillOpacity`
-   `stroke`
-   `group`
-   `label`

Let's iterate and make some improvements!

@fig-black shows what happens we just change the color from blue to black. 

```{r}
#| label: fig-black 
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map with SoCalEJ census tracts color changed to black
#| echo: true
#| 
leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ, 
              color = 'black') #NEW 

```

The lines are too heavy. Let's lower the `weight` - we'll let the color go back to blue to keep the example minimal.

@fig-weight shows the result, which is a much cleaner look.

```{r}
#| label: fig-weight
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map with line weight changed to 1.
#| echo: true

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ, 
              color = 'black',
              weight = 1)  #NEW

```

#### Visualization Iteration 

Within the SoCalEJ dataset are over 50 columns of values with names like "Ozone", "Pesticide", and "DieselPM_P". We will use the fillcolor variable to indicate the values of one of these columns using a color palette.

Functions that help define the color palette in `leaflet` are:

-   `colorNumeric()` - a linear color scale
-   `colorQuantile()` - a color scale that can ensure the bin sizes are approximately equal
-   `colorFactor()` - a color scale that is good for categorical ('non-numeric') data

```{r}
#| label: assign color palette for DieselPM_P
#| echo: true

#NEW - color palette definition
pal1 <- colorNumeric(palette = 'magma', domain = SoCalEJ$DieselPM_P) 

```

Now we see what those two palettes look like in the map. @fig-numeric shows the default numeric palette, but it is too faint with the background tiles.

```{r}
#| label: fig-numeric
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map for Ozone in Southern California.
#| echo: true

leaflet() |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  addPolygons(data = SoCalEJ,
              weight = 1,
              color = ~pal1(DieselPM_P) #NEW
              ) 

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-legend
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map for with numeric palette for Diesel PM percentiles, color legend, and opacity optimized.
#| echo: true

leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> #NEW 
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron)|> 
  addPolygons(color = ~pal1(DieselPM_P),
              weight = 1,
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = pal1, 
            title = 'Diesel Particulate Matter (%)', 
            values = ~DieselPM_P)

```

The `SoCalEJ` dataset has multiple columns that have values of `-999` indicating missing data which will interfere with color scales.

@fig-Lead_P is a map using the `Lead_P` variable instead of DieselPM_P. Lead is an indication of lead-based paint exposures based on housing age households with children under age 6.  

```{r}
#| label: fig-Lead_P
#| fig-cap: Leaflet map for Lead Paint exposure percentile in Southern California
#| echo: true

palLead <- colorNumeric(palette = 'PuOr', domain = SoCalEJ$Lead_P,
                        na.color = '#808080')

leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> 
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) |> 
  setView(lat = 34, lng = -117.60, zoom = 9) |> 
  addPolygons(weight = 1,
              color = ~palLead(Lead_P),
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = palLead, 
            title = 'Lead (%)', 
            values = ~Lead_P)

```


The -999 values make the visualization useless.  We need to clean the dataset. A function can replace -999 values with `NA`.  

## Functions

A function can be created by defining a name, arguments, the code to execute the algorithm, and a return of the transformed dataset.

```{r}
#| label: Functional example
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# General Structure
function_name <- function(data, argument1) {
  # Perform cleaning steps (e.g., replace -999 with NA)
  cleaned_data <- ... 
  return(cleaned_data)
}

```

### Very Simple Example 

```{webr}
add_number <- function(x,y) {
  x + y
}
# zip code of Scripps college and number of students
add_number(91711, 1137)
```

#### Functional exercises

1.  Try to design your own function called `multiply_number`
2.  Try to design your own function that prints a user name that they enter.  

### Very complicated function

R has some non-standard issues when working with data frames.  I'll show this example but breaking it down is pretty advanced.  

Start with a working example of replacing -999 values in the data frame.

The `mutate()` function creates a new variable.
The `if_else()` function checks to see if a condition is TRUE or FALSE, then returns the second value if TRUE and the third value if FALSE.

```{r}
#| label: Practical Example
#| echo: true

SoCalEJ2 <- mutate(SoCalEJ, 
    # this searches for -999 values and replaces them with -999 for the Lead_P variable
            clean = if_else(Lead_P == -999, NA, Lead_P)
      )

```

This works!  

However, to create the function that applies that code, we need to use non-standard evaluation embrace and some quotes to ensure proper evaluation within the code.  This is an unsuitable example for beginners, but it does what I want to demonstrate in terms of quality controlling the table.  

```{r}
#| label: Function replace999
#| echo: true

# name and arguments
replace999 <- function(df, var) {
  clean <- mutate(df, 
    "clean" = if_else({{ var }} == -999, NA, {{ var }}))
  # mutate() defines a new variable
  
  return(clean)
}

```

### Apply the function

```{r}
#| label: apply replace999 function
#| echo: true

SoCalEJ <- replace999(SoCalEJ, Lead_P) 

```

This part is simple.  

### Rerun the map code

@fig-clean_Lead shows the update

```{r}
#| label: fig-clean_Lead
#| fig-cap: Clean Lead Paint exposure percentile in Southern California
#| echo: true

##Note - the column name is now clean rather than Lead_P in the color palette and code
palLead <- colorNumeric(palette = 'magma', domain = SoCalEJ$clean,  
                        na.color = '#808080')

leaflet(data = SoCalEJ) |> 
  addTiles() |> 
  setView(lat = 34, lng = -117.60, zoom = 9) |> 
  addPolygons(stroke = FALSE,
              fillColor = ~palLead(clean),
              fillOpacity = 0.8) |> 
  addLegend(pal = palLead, 
            title = 'Lead Paint (%)', 
            values = ~clean)

```
